current.title: Idol
current.note: been on repeat for two weeks straight. the production is immaculate and the dual vocal performance is genuinely unreal.

playlists.name: late night mode
playlists.description: lo-fi, ambient, slow electronic. for 3am when the world has finally gone quiet and your brain won't stop.

playlists.name: boss fight energy
playlists.description: full send. for gaming sessions, creative sprints, or just when you need to feel completely invincible.
tracks: Unravel — TK from Ling Tosite Sigure
Libera Me From Hell — Gurren Lagann OST

playlists.name: cozy morning
playlists.description: soft, warm, light. coffee not optional. good for slow starts, reading, and pretending you're in a café.

playlists.name: emotional damage
playlists.description: OSTs and sad bangers. for when you want to feel something very deeply. you have been warned.
tracks: A Cruel Angel's Thesis — Yoko Takahashi

writeups.name: YOASOBI
writeups.blurb: Literature-to-music adaptations done with surgical precision. Ikura's vocals are technically flawless and emotionally devastating in equal measure. Ayase's production makes every song feel like a controlled collapse — it shouldn't work as well as it does.

writeups.name: Joe Hisaishi
writeups.blurb: The reason I can't watch Ghibli films without crying. My Neighbor Totoro alone would cement his legacy forever. Merry-Go-Round of Life is one of the most perfect pieces of music ever written and I will not be taking questions.

writeups.name: Kenshi Yonezu
writeups.blurb: Ghost of his HACHI era but in the best possible way — mainstream without losing the weirdness. Lemon is a masterclass in pop songwriting. Kick Back made me watch Chainsaw Man twice just to hear it in context.

writeups.name: NieR:Automata OST
writeups.blurb: Keiichi Okabe composed an entire emotional journey disguised as a game soundtrack. Weight of the World hits different once you understand what it's actually saying. The full choir version is a religious experience regardless of whether you've played the game.
